crossref.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>The ideal mechanical properties and behaviors of materials without the influence of defects are of great fundamental and engineering significance but considered inaccessible. Here, we use single-atom-thin isotopically pure hexagonal boron nitride (<jats:italic>h</jats:italic>BN) to demonstrate that two-dimensional (2D) materials offer us close-to ideal experimental platforms to study intrinsic mechanical phenomena. The highly delicate isotope effect on the mechanical properties of monolayer <jats:italic>h</jats:italic>BN is directly measured by indentation: lighter <jats:sup>10</jats:sup>B gives rise to higher elasticity and strength than heavier <jats:sup>11</jats:sup>B. This anomalous isotope effect establishes that the intrinsic mechanical properties without the effect of defects could be measured, and the so-called ultrafine and normally neglected isotopic perturbation in nuclear charge distribution sometimes plays a more critical role than the isotopic mass effect in the mechanical and other physical properties of materials.</jats:p>
title: Anomalous isotope effect on mechanical properties of single atomic layer Boron Nitride
